lesson.menu_desc: Conditional Clauses with if / unless / provided -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 12 -- Reading Contracts II -- Conditional Clauses
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Simone Quiles de Santana Marques | Aula 12 -- Reading Contracts II

listenings.text: Hi Simone, this is Daniel again from the legal team. Thank you for the notes on the clauses last week. The German partner sent the new draft, and I want you to read the conditions before we meet. There is one provision that worries me. It says the contract ends if either party fails to pay within thirty days. I think that is too strict, so I would like a softer condition. Please check the governing law as well. The draft says the contract follows German jurisdiction, but I would prefer Brazilian courts if we can agree. One more thing: there is a waiver clause that lets a party give up a right, and we should only accept it provided it is in writing. If the partner agrees to these points, we can sign. Unless they agree, we wait. Let us talk after you read it. Thank you, and talk soon.

Hello, this is Simone with my notes on the new draft, before our review with the partner. First, the contract terminates if a party does not pay within thirty days, and I agree with Daniel that this is too strict. I will ask for sixty days, provided the partner accepts a small interest. Second, the draft follows German jurisdiction, but I would like Brazilian courts, unless the partner has a strong reason. Third, there is a provision that lets us amend the contract, but only in writing, which is good. Finally, I will not accept the waiver unless it is signed by both parties, because a right that you give up by accident is dangerous. If the partner agrees, we enforce the contract from June. I will raise these points at our meeting on Thursday.

extra_audio.text: Daniel sends the new draft and asks Simone to read the conditions. Simone reads the provision that ends the contract if a party does not pay in thirty days. She decides to ask for sixty days, provided the partner accepts a small interest. She marks the governing law, because she prefers Brazilian courts unless the partner has a strong reason. At the review meeting, Simone proposes the new conditions to the other party.
